coredata.dc:title: Ultra-sensitive mercury detection from surface wrapping of carbon dots reduced and decorated silver nanoparticles on SERS fiber probes
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Xiaoyu
Wang, Yan
Wang, Tianyi
Zhang, Jihong
Xie, Jun
Han, Jianjun
Zhao, Xiujian
coredata.dc:description: Highly selective and sensitive detection of toxic mercury ions (Hg2+) has become a critical issue in recent decades, due to increasing environmental and health requirements. Herein, carbon dots reduced and decorated Ag (CDs/Ag) nanoparticles based fiber probes were fabricated, providing a “turn-off” surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) for low-concentration Hg2+ ions detection. The CDs/Ag nanoparticles fiber probe exhibits better SERS performance than that of Ag nanoparticles fiber, attributed to the stronger electromagnetic intensities induced by localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) and larger hot-spot areas resulting from the interaction between silver nanoparticles and carbon dots, under 532 nm beam excitation. As low as 10−11 M Hg2+ ions can be detected through the decrease in Raman scattering intensities of CV molecules, due to the SERS inactive Hg metal layer wrapping the surface of silver nanoparticles, through the reduction of Hg2+ ions by carbon dots. The dropping of silver nanoparticles’ LSPR induced absorption peak, EDS elemental mapping, and XPS analysis all confirm that the wrapping decreases the LSPR effect and corresponding Raman intensity. Furthermore, the detection shows high selectivity and can only be applied for detecting Hg2+ ions, owing to the reduction capability of carbon dots. The close analysis results obtained using SERS fiber probes and commercial inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) for detecting Hg2+ ions in real water sample confirm the reliability of this detection method. This research indicates the CDs/Ag fiber probe has a great potential application for environmental Hg2+ ions detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: SERS
Mercury ions
Carbon dots
Silver nanoparticles
Surface wrapping

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: SERS performance of CDs/Ag nanoparticles fiber probe

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was financially supported by, the project of Sanya Yazhou Bay Science and Technology City ( SCKJ-JYRC-2022-44 ) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( WUT: 2023III007XXH ). The author Jihong Zhang would like to thank Ms. Utada Hikaru, for her beautiful songs inspired this research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption spectra, inset: digital images of CDs and CDs/Ag nanoparticles solutions; (b) XRD patterns of synthesized CDs and CDs/Ag nanoparticles; (c) TEM image of CDs, inset: HR-TEM image with fringe spacing; (d) TEM image of CDs/Ag nanoparticles, inset: size distribution of CDs/Ag nanoparticles and SAED image; (e) Amplified TEM image of CDs/Ag nanoparticles, inset: HR-TEM image with fringe spacing; (f) HAADF image; (g) Ag element mapping and (h) C element mapping.

(a) Full-range XPS spectra of CDs and CDs/Ag nanoparticles and high-resolution spectra of (b) C 1 s, (c) O 1 s, (d) N 1 s in CDs, and (e) Ag 3d, (f) C 1 s, (g) O 1 s, (h) N 1 s in CDs/Ag nanoparticles.

(a) Raman spectra of 10−5 M CV using CDs, Ag, CDs/Ag nanoparticles based fiber probes; (b) Raman spectra of CV molecules with concentrations from 10−4 M to 10−8 M using Ag fiber probes, under 532 nm laser excitation; (c) Raman spectra CV molecules with concentrations from 10−5 M to 10−10 M using CDs/Ag fiber probes, under 532 nm laser excitation; (d) Intensities of 1619 cm−1 peak variation according to CV concentrations, using Ag nanoparticles fiber probes; (e) Intensities of 1619 cm−1 peak variation according to CV concentrations, using CDs/Ag nanoparticles fiber probes.

Simulations of electric field distribution of (a) isolated Ag nanoparticle, (b) double Ag nanoparticles with 10 nm gap, (c) isolated CDs/Ag nanoparticle, inset: amplified electric field distribution at the interface of CDs and Ag nanoparticle, and (d) double CDs/Ag nanoparticles with 10 nm gap.

(a) Raman spectra of 10−5 M CV molecules from CDs/Ag SERS fiber probes with different Hg2+ concentrations; (b) Intensity reduction rate of peak at 1619 cm−1 variation according to Hg2+ concentration; (c) Normalized SERS intensity at 1619 cm−1 from CDs/Ag nanoparticles fiber probes with different metal ions.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs/Ag nanoparticles solution with and without 10−4 M Hg2+; (b) Full-range XPS spectra, detailed high-resolution XPS spectra of (c) C 1 s and (d) Hg 4 f from mixed CDs/Ag nanoparticles and 10−4 M Hg2+ solution; (e) TEM image of CDs/Ag nanoparticle interacted with Hg2+, inset: HR-TEM image with fringe spacing at the core and edge areas; (f) Ag element mapping; (g) Hg element mapping; (h) Ag and Hg element mapping of CDs/Ag nanoparticles interacted with Hg2+.
The comparison of the LOD for Hg2+ ions detection with different methods.
The measured Hg2+ concentrations using SERS and ICP-MS.

head.ce:title: Ultra-sensitive mercury detection from surface wrapping of carbon dots reduced and decorated silver nanoparticles on SERS fiber probes

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures, Wuhan University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly selective and sensitive detection of toxic mercury ions (Hg2+) has become a critical issue in recent decades, due to increasing environmental and health requirements. Herein, carbon dots reduced and decorated Ag (CDs/Ag) nanoparticles based fiber probes were fabricated, providing a “turn-off” surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) for low-concentration Hg2+ ions detection. The CDs/Ag nanoparticles fiber probe exhibits better SERS performance than that of Ag nanoparticles fiber, attributed to the stronger electromagnetic intensities induced by localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) and larger hot-spot areas resulting from the interaction between silver nanoparticles and carbon dots, under 532 nm beam excitation. As low as 10−11 M Hg2+ ions can be detected through the decrease in Raman scattering intensities of CV molecules, due to the SERS inactive Hg metal layer wrapping the surface of silver nanoparticles, through the reduction of Hg2+ ions by carbon dots. The dropping of silver nanoparticles’ LSPR induced absorption peak, EDS elemental mapping, and XPS analysis all confirm that the wrapping decreases the LSPR effect and corresponding Raman intensity. Furthermore, the detection shows high selectivity and can only be applied for detecting Hg2+ ions, owing to the reduction capability of carbon dots. The close analysis results obtained using SERS fiber probes and commercial inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) for detecting Hg2+ ions in real water sample confirm the reliability of this detection method. This research indicates the CDs/Ag fiber probe has a great potential application for environmental Hg2+ ions detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Highly toxic mercury ions (Hg2+) can accumulate in environment and further in human body through food chains, causing permanent damage even at low concentrations [1]. Thus, the development of ultrasensitive Hg2+ detection methods has become an important issue in pollution precaution and has attracted tremendous attention in past decades. Various analytical methods, such as atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [2], fluorescence spectrometry [3], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [4,5], and colorimetry [6,7], have been developed to detect Hg2+ ions. However, these methods are either time-consuming, low-sensitivity, or complicated operation processes, which limit their practical application. Therefore, it is essential to develop an ultra-sensitive, highly selective, and low-cost method for Hg2+ ions detection.

Surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) has emerged as a promising candidate for ultra-sensitive Hg2+ ions detection, as it can amplify Raman signals by orders of magnitude. The amplification originates from electromagnetic enhancement (EM) and chemical enhancement (CE) mechanisms. EM plays a significant contribution to SERS, primarily related to the electromagnetic field generated by localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) of metallic nanostructures upon laser excitation [8]. CE arises from the chemical bonds formation, resonance, and light induced charge transfer between metal nanostructures and analyte molecules [9,10]. Generally, noble metal nanomaterials (Au, Ag, Cu) are extensively used as SERS substrates. Among them, Ag is widely regarded as the most ideal substrate due to its remarkable LSPR performance in the visible range [11,12]. In recent years, some SERS-based methods have been reported for Hg2+ ions detection. For example, Hg2+ ions coordinate with organic analyte molecules, such as 4,4′-dipyridyl [13], or thymine [14] molecules, inducing the “turn on” or “turn off” of Raman scattering intensities [15]. The selection of appropriate organic coordinators as detection markers is extremely important for these methods, as it directly influences the detection performance of SERS sensors. It is necessary to develop a highly sensitive, selective, and stable SERS detection method for Hg2+ ions with a common analysis marker.

Carbon dots (CDs), a carbon-based fluorescence material with a diameter of less than 10 nm, with rich oxygen-containing groups on surface, can serve as excellent electron donors and reducing agents. In recent years, many researchers have investigated carbon dot/noble metal nanocomposites as SERS substrates for organic molecules or heavy metal ions detection. For example, Tang et al. confirmed that CDs modified Ag nanoparticles can selectively detect atrazine with a detection limit of 10−8 M from the SERS intensity variation of rhodamine 6 G marker [16]. Luo et al. used carbon dots as a reducing agent to synthesize gold@carbon dot composite nanoparticles (Au@CDs), achieving a 4 times higher enhancement factor than that of gold nanoparticles [17]. This enhancement can be attributed to the combination of CDs with plasmonic materials, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and plasmon coupling leading to an increase in the electron density of metal nanoparticles. Furthermore, the LSPR effect and SERS performance of metal nanostructures can be improved [18].

Optical fibers as optical transmission media or sensing platforms have been widely used for SERS chemical or biological detection, due to their great flexibility, lightweight, low loss and remote in-situ sensing capabilities [19–21]. By integrating optical fibers with SERS materials, SERS fiber probes can greatly lower the detection limit of optical fiber [22,23], which provides a broad potential application for long-distance in situ detection of liquid samples and biological samples.

In current research, novel CDs/Ag SERS fiber probes were fabricated and applied for trace Hg2+ ions detection. Silver nanoparticles were synthesized by directly reducing AgNO3 with CDs, and decorated with CDs through electrostatic attraction. The SERS performance was evaluated using crystal violet (CV) as the probe molecules. The detection performances of trace Hg2+ ions, including the detection limit, selectivity, and mechanism, were studied. The relationship between Hg2+ concentrations and SERS intensities and the elemental distribution of nanostructures suggest that the mercury layer reduced by CDs wraps around the Ag nanoparticles, thereby “turning off’’ the Raman enhancement. Moreover, the fiber probes were successfully applied for the accurate detection of Hg2+ ions in real water samples. This work will provide a potentially highly sensitive, selective, qualitative and quantitative detection method for Hg2+ in complex systems.

Silver nitrate and ethylenediamine for the synthesis of Ag nanoparticles and CDs were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd, China. Glucose (98%) for CDs preparation, triethoxy-3-aminopropylsilane (ATPES) for fiber probes fabrication, crystal violet (99.5%), mercury standard solution (1000 μg/mL), cadmium standard solution (1 mg/mL), arsenic standard solution (1000 μg/mL), zinc standard solution (1000 μg/mL), lead standard solution (1000 μg/mL), barium standard solution (1000 μg/mL), cobalt standard solution (100 μg/mL) and copper standard solution (1000 μg/mL) for SERS performance characterization, were purchased from Aladdin, Shanghai, China. All the chemicals were used as received without further purification. Deionized (DI) water with 18.2 MΩ·cm resistivity as solvent was produced by a Dura 12 ultrapure-water purification system from Nihon Millipore Ltd., Tokyo, Japan. 125 µm multi-mode silica optical fibers with 62.5 µm core diameter were purchased from Yangtze Optical Fiber & Cable Joint Stock Co. Ltd, Wuhan, China.

CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method following the protocol from Tang et al. [16], with slight modifications. In brief, 6.000 g of glucose was weighed and added to 40 mL of DI water, and completely dissolved at room temperature (25 ℃). Then, 230 µL of ethylenediamine was added to the solution and placed in a 90 ℃ water bath with magnetic stirring at 1000 rpm for 30 min. Subsequently, the solution was transferred into a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 10 h, then cooled to room temperature. The obtained suspension solution was filtered with a 0.22 µm microporous membrane to remove large particles. The CDs solution was placed into a refrigerator for further use.

CDs/Ag composite nanoparticles were synthesized through direct reduction of Ag+ ions by CDs and decorated with CDs on the surface. In detail, 0.170 g of AgNO3 was dissolved in 30 mL of DI water, and 0.5 mL of CDs solution was added into the solution and reacted at 90 ℃ under 500 rpm magnetic stirring for 2 h. The obtained brown solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm and washed with DI water three times to obtain CDs/Ag composite nanoparticles. Subsequently, the nanoparticles were dispersed into 5 mL of DI water for further characterization and fiber probes fabrication. The obtained CDs/Ag nanoparticles show good dispersion and stability in DI water.
The detailed synthesis procedure of regular Ag nanoparticles, as a reference, can be found in supporting information.

Fiber probes were fabricated by homogeneously coating the nanoparticles on the fiber end-tip facet. The optical fibers were silanized by immersing fiber tips in a 5 µL/mL of APTES aqueous solution for 30 min, then rapidly transferred to CDs/Ag solution and immersed for 24 h. Finally, the fibers were rinsed several times with anhydrous ethanol.

The absorption spectra of CDs and CDs/Ag nanoparticles were obtained by a UV-Vis-NIR spectrometer (UV-Vis-NIR 3600, Shidamazu, Japan), in the range of 250–800 nm. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of nanostructures were recorded using an X-ray diffractometer (D8 Discover, Bruker, Germany) with a Cu Kα X-ray source (1.5418 Å), 2°/min scanning speed, and 0.02° resolution. The morphologies, sizes of the synthesized materials, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), and element distributions were analyzed using a Talos F200s transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI, USA) equipped with selected area electron diffraction (SAED), with a 200 kV acceleration voltage. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Escalab 250 Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The distribution of composite nanoparticles on the optical fiber end-tip surface was observed using a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, S-4800, Hitachi, Japan). The surface potentials of CDs, Ag nanoparticles and CDs/Ag composite nanoparticles were analyzed using a zeta potential analyzer (Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Malvern, UK).

Fiber probes were immersed in different concentrations of crystal violet (CV) solutions for SERS performance evaluation. Raman spectra of CV molecules were obtained using a Raman spectrometer (LABHRev-UV, Horiba, Japan), under 10.6 mW 532 nm laser excitation, with around 2 µm focus beam diameter, 4 s integration time, and 3 s accumulation duration. The Hg2+ ions were measured through the changes of CV Raman spectra intensities, which resulted from the CDs reducing Hg2+ ions into metallic mercury and wrapping around CDs/Ag nanoparticles on fiber probes. In detail, 2 mL of different concentrations of Hg2+ solutions (by gradually diluting the mercury standard solution) or DI water were mixed with CDs/Ag aqueous solution. Then the fiber probes were immersed into the mixed solution for 24 h to absorb molecules for Raman measurement. The Raman spectra were measured using the above protocols, with identical parameters. The quantitative analysis was performed from the variation of 1619 cm−1 peak intensities of CV Raman spectra. All measurements were conducted at room temperature.

The electromagnetic field distributions of nanostructures were simulated using finite element method (FEM) in COMSOL® Multiphysics 6.0 software package. The diameters of 3D simulation models for CD and Ag nanoparticle were 2 nm and 40 nm, respectively, following the synthesized nanoparticle diameters. The gap between Ag dimers was 10 nm, following the distribution of nanoparticles on the fiber probe shown in the SEM image (Fig. S2). A planar wave with 532 nm wavelength was chosen as the incident light source, propagating along the z-axis and polarizing along the x-axis. The electric field intensity was 1 V/m and the refractive index of background (air medium) was 1. The dielectric constants of CDs and Ag were chosen from Phillip and Taft [24] and Johnson and Christy [25] experiment data, respectively.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs and CDs/Ag nanoparticles solutions are shown in Fig. 1a. The yellow-brown CDs aqueous solution (Fig. 1a inset) shows two absorption peaks, the peak centered at 280 nm can be attributed to the π-π * transition of the sp2 aromatic carbon atom, while the peak at 340 nm corresponds to the n-π * transition of the C=O, C=N and C-N bonds [26,27]. In brown CDs/Ag nanoparticles solution (Fig. 1a inset), a characteristic absorption peak at 430 nm is associated with the characteristic LSPR absorption band of Ag nanoparticles [29]. However, the attenuation of the CDs absorption peaks is probably due to the overlap with the absorption peaks of Ag nanoparticles and their attachment to the surface of Ag nanoparticles [30]. The CDs exhibit typical photoluminescence behavior in Fig. S1, which can be attributed to the surface state of CDs [28]. Additionally, the fluorescence is quenched after the formation of CDs/Ag composite nanoparticles (Fig. S1). The XRD pattern of CDs shows a broad halo centered at around 2θ = 25°, attributed to the (0 0 2) lattice plane of graphite, confirming the graphitized structure [31] and small sizes of the obtained CDs. The XRD pattern of CDs/Ag nanoparticles exhibits characteristic diffraction peaks at 38.2°, 44.3°, 64.5°, 77.5° and 82.5°, corresponding to the (1 1 1), (2 0 0), (2 2 0), (3 1 1) and (2 2 2) lattice planes of face-centered cubic (fcc) Ag [JCPDS 04–0783], along with a broad peak at 25° from CDs (Fig. 1b). The TEM image of CDs (Fig. 1c) shows numerous spherical nanoparticles with an average size of about 2 nm and the lattice distance (Fig. 1c inset) of 0.21 nm corresponds to the (1 0 0) crystal plane of graphite. As shown in Fig. 1d, the CDs/Ag nanoparticles (Fig. 1d) synthesized by direct reduction of AgNO3 from CDs show a spherical morphology with a diameter of about 40 nm, and the size distribution follows a Gaussian distribution (Fig. 1d inset). The SAED image of CDs/Ag nanoparticles (Fig. 1d inset) corresponds to (1 1 1), (2 0 0), (2 2 0) and (3 1 1) crystal planes of Ag, indicating the formation of face-centered cubic Ag crystals. Notably, the graphite crystal planes on the SAED images cannot be distinguished due to the low content and small diameters of CDs. Moreover, another magnified TEM image (Fig. 1e) confirms that the surface of Ag nanoparticle is wrapped with CDs. The 0.23 nm lattice distance (Fig. 1e inset) corresponds to the (1 1 1) plane of the face-centered cubic Ag structure. High-angle annular dark-filed scanning TEM (HAADF-STEM) of the nanoparticles and corresponding Ag, C elements distributions from EDS mapping (Fig. 1f-h) further confirm the formation of CDs decorated Ag nanoparticles. Furthermore, it is reasonable to propose that the CDs are uniformly distributed on Ag nanoparticles based on the homogeneous C element distribution. The effect of carbon film on copper grid during TEM measurement has been removed as background.

In order to further confirm the compositions of synthesized CDs and CDs/Ag nanoparticles, XPS analysis was conducted. The full-range XPS spectrum of CDs/Ag nanoparticles shows typical peaks for the C, N, and O elements of CDs, along with distinct Ag characteristic peaks, while the Si peak originates from the silicon substrate for measurement. For CDs, the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s ( Fig. 2b-d) show peaks at 284.8, 286.2, 287.9, 289.0 eV, which can be attributed to C-C/CC, C-N, C-O and CO groups, respectively [32,33]. Peaks at 531.7 and 532.4 eV represent CO and C-O groups, respectively [34]. Furthermore, peaks at 399.6, 400.1 and 401.5 eV can be assigned to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively [35,36]. For CDs/Ag nanoparticles, a pair of peaks at 369.7 eV (Ag 3d5/2) and 375.1 eV (Ag 3d3/2) associated with the binding energies of the Ag(0) (Fig. 2e). Additionally, the information regarding the C, O, and N elements is similar to that of CDs (Fig. 2f-h), except for a strong peak at 406.8 eV from -NO2 group [37] in N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2h), probably generating from the addition of AgNO3. Compared to CDs, the decreased ratio of oxygenated groups (C-O, CO) in CDs/Ag nanoparticles (Fig. 2b, f) further confirms the reduction of Ag+ to Ag metal by CDs [38]. Furthermore, the abundant oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups on the surface of CDs/Ag nanoparticles allow for the connection of various molecules and facilitate SERS detection. Moreover, zeta potential measurements (Fig. S2) show that CDs (+31.4 mV) and Ag nanoparticles synthesized by traditional reduction (−23.2 mV) possess opposite surface charges, suggesting that the CDs and Ag nanoparticles can be attracted through anisotropic charge, forming CDs/Ag nanoparticles with a final positive surface charge (+9.52 mV).

ce:section.ce:section-title: SERS performance of CDs/Ag nanoparticles fiber probe
ce:section.ce:para: SERS fiber probes were fabricated by coating CDs/Ag nanoparticles on the fiber end-tip facet. SEM images (Fig. S3) show that the nanostructures are homogeneously and densely distributed on the fiber surface.

Due to the well-known Raman features and large Raman scattering cross section [39,40], CV was used as the probe molecules to evaluate the SERS performance of fiber probes. As shown in Fig. 3a, the Raman scattering peaks match well with the standard CV. The characteristic peaks at 917, 1172, and 1390 cm−1 can be attributed to ring skeletal vibration of radical orientation, in-plane vibration of ring C-H, and N-phenyl stretching, respectively, while the characteristic peaks at 1584 and 1619 cm−1 are assigned to ring C-C stretching [41,42]. Fiber probe with CDs/Ag nanoparticles shows much stronger Raman intensity than that with Ag nanoparticles, while the fiber probe only with CDs doesn’t exhibit distinct Raman scattering peaks, using 10−5 M CV as analyte, suggesting that CDs can improve the SERS performance of Ag nanoparticles. To further evaluate the SERS performance, fiber probes with CDs/Ag nanoparticles and Ag nanoparticles were used to detect different concentrations of CV. The Raman intensities decrease as CV concentrations decrease (Fig. 3b, c). The lowest detection limit can be 10−7 M for the fiber probe with Ag nanoparticles, and 10−9 M for the fiber probe with CDs/Ag nanoparticles, following the principle of a signal-to-noise ratio higher than 3. Moreover, the relationship between CV concentrations and Raman peak intensities (1619 cm−1) exhibits a linear correlation for both the probes with Ag nanoparticles and CDs/Ag nanoparticles (Fig. 3d, e), indicating the potential for quantitative analysis. The calculated enhancement factor (EF) of CDs/Ag fiber probes can reach 1.4 × 107, which is 3.9 times higher than that of Ag fiber probes (3.6 × 106). (The detailed calculation process can be found in supporting information). This phenomenon arises from the combination of Ag nanoparticles and CDs, incurring the FRET process and plasmon coupling. As a result, the electron density on the surface increases and generates an enhanced local electric field of Ag nanoparticles [18].

The electromagnetic field was simulated by FEM to further analyze the enhancement. Under 532 nm beam excitation, the double Ag nanoparticles ( Fig. 4b) exhibit a higher maximum electric field intensity (7.67 V/m) compared to the isolated Ag nanoparticles (Fig. 4a) (3.83 V/m), which can be attributed to the space effect [43]. Moreover, the introduction of CDs decoration significantly enhances the electric field intensity induced by LSPR, reaching a maximum value of 54.78 V/m (Fig. 4c), and further increases for double CDs/Ag nanoparticles (61.63 V/m) (Fig. 4d). The maximum electric field appears at the nanogap between CDs and Ag nanoparticle (Fig. 4c inset). Therefore, the strong electric field and large hot-spot area may be the reason for the good SERS property of CDs/Ag nanoparticle based fiber probes.

Moreover, the fiber probes show excellent reproducibility and stability in Fig. S4, with less than 5% difference in measurement data from the same batch probes using 10−6 M CV as analyte. Even after 28 days of exposure to ambient atmosphere, the SERS peak remains distinguishable, albeit with a 70% signal loss compared to the freshly prepared fiber probe, using 10−7 M CV as analyte.

The dependence of CV Raman intensities on Hg2+ ions concentrations ( Fig. 5a) shows a negative correlation, which is opposite to other traditional positive analyte concentration-dependent detection methods. Compared to the Raman spectrum of Hg2+ free (0 M) sample, the Raman intensity exhibits a 10% reduction for 10−11 M Hg2+ ions, while about a 5% reduction for 10−12 M Hg2+ ions (Fig. 5b). Considering a possible ± 5% Raman measurement error, 10−11 M is regarded as the lowest detection limit, which is much lower than the maximum concentration of Hg2+ in drinking water stipulated by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) (2 μg/L, 10−8 M) [44]. In fact, compared with the latest reported Hg2+ ions detection methods, such as AAS, fluorescence, ICP-MS, colorimetry and SERS with special markers, this SERS sensor reaches a lower LOD without marker molecules, as listed in Table 1. Moreover, the Raman peaks almost disappear with 10−4 M Hg2+ ions, which represents the highest detectable concentration. The relationship between Hg2+ concentrations and reduction rates of Raman intensity at 1619 cm−1 shows good linearity, indicating the potential for quantitative analysis of Hg2+ ions (Fig. 5b). Moreover, the slope of linear relationship confirms the good sensitivity of fiber probes.

Selectivity of this method was evaluated by examining other common heavy metal ions, including Ba2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Cd2+, As3+ and Cu2+, all at the same concentration as Hg2+ (10−5 M). As shown in Fig. 5c, only the Raman intensity at 1619 cm−1 with Hg2+ ions presents an obvious drop, while the intensities with other ions remain almost unchanged, probably due to the higher redox potential of mercury (+0.92 V) than other metal ions (Table S1), which can be reduced by carbon dots. The high selectivity of the measurement method indicates the potential application of CDs/Ag fiber probes for detecting Hg2+ ions in complex solutions containing multiple types of metal ions.

In order to investigate the interaction between CDs/Ag nanoparticles and Hg2+ ions, as well as the origin of Raman intensity decrease, 1 mL of 10−4 M Hg2+ solution was added to 1 mL of prepared CDs/Ag nanoparticles aqueous solution. After adding Hg2+ ions solution ( Fig. 6a), the LSPR-induced absorption peak of CDs/Ag nanoparticles, centered at 430 nm, significantly diminishes and nearly vanishes. In addition, the solution turns colorless (Fig. 6a inset), indicating a transformation of the nanoparticles. This further results in a sharp decay of Raman spectra intensities of CV analyte from fiber probes. Meanwhile, a new peak centered at 300 nm appears, which can be attributed to the absorption peak of metallic mercury [57,58]. The XPS analysis (Fig. 6b) confirms the presence of Hg component (peaks at 100.3 eV and 104.5 eV) in nanoparticles (Fig. 6d), along with C, N, O and Ag elements. According to the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s (Fig. 6c), the ratio of C-O and CO is 7.19%, which is lower than that of CDs (29.61%) and CDs/Ag nanoparticles (17.02%). In this case, CDs act as a reducing agent, leading to the further oxidation and removal of some oxygenated groups after the addition of Hg2+ ions [38]. Moreover, there are still Hg2+ ions exist (peaks at 101.6 eV and 105.9 eV) that may be absorbed on the nanoparticle surface, due to too high Hg2+ ions concentration to be completely reduced by CDs (Fig. 6d). Furthermore, the TEM image of CDs/Ag nanoparticles with Hg2+ ions also shows a core/shell-like structure (Fig. 6e), with lattice spacings of 0.228 nm and 0.235 nm at the center and edge of the nanoparticles, respectively. This suggests the possible presence of Ag and Hg elements. However, it is difficult to distinguish Hg from Ag due to the close lattice distances of the (1 1 0) plane of mercury (0.227 nm) and the (1 1 1) plane of silver (0.235 nm). The EDS elemental mappings further confirm the presence of both silver and mercury in the nanoparticles, with solid spherical silver particle (Fig. 6f), hollow mercury structure (Fig. 6g), and silver-core-mercury-shell nanoparticle (Fig. 6h).

CDs act as a reducing agent to reduce Ag+ to metallic Ag, and further grow Ag nanoparticles. The residual CDs can attach to Ag nanoparticles for CDs/Ag structure formation. Upon interaction with Hg2+ ions, the CDs on the surface of Ag nanoparticles reduce Hg2+ to Hg (0) and metallic Hg wraps around Ag nanoparticles. The formation of SERS inactive Hg shell decreases the LSPR effect of Ag nanoparticles and further decays the Raman intensity of analyte. Moreover, the wrapping also leads to the indirect absorption of analyte on Ag nanoparticles, which further decreases the Raman intensity.

To evaluate the feasibility of CDs/Ag fiber probes in real water sample detection, the Hg2+ ions in lake water were detected. The lake water was collected from Wuhan University of Technology campus and filtered using a 0.22 µm microporous membrane to remove suspended particles. Since Hg2+ ions cannot be detected using SERS fiber probe and ICP-MS ( Table 2), extra known concentrations of Hg2+ ions were added into the lake water for detection. As shown in Table 2, the Hg2+ concentrations detected by the SERS fiber probes closely match those by the ICP-MS method. The recoveries of Hg2+ ions range from 91.2% to 112.0% with an RSD of 1.1–5.0%. The result indicates that the SERS fiber probes have a promising application for real samples.

In conclusion, a novel “turn off” SERS method for low-concentration toxic Hg2+ ions detection has been developed, based on the surface wrapping of Hg shell on CDs/Ag nanoparticles. Ag nanoparticles with a diameter of about 40 nm were synthesized via CDs direct reduction of AgNO3 method, and CDs were decorated on the surface of Ag nanoparticle through electrostatic attraction to form CDs/Ag composite nanoparticles. The optical properties, crystal phases, morphologies, structures, and distribution of nanoparticles were confirmed by UV-Vis-NIR, XRD, TEM, and EDS mapping analysis. The CDs/Ag nanoparticles based fiber probes were fabricated by coating nanoparticles on the silica fiber end-tip facet. The fiber probes show better SERS performance, with the lowest detection limit of 10−9 M for CV molecules, which is two orders of magnitude lower than that of the Ag nanoparticles fiber probes, resulting from stronger LSPR induced electromagnetic field from the interaction between CDs and Ag nanoparticles. The linear relationship between CV concentrations and Raman peak intensities, good reproducibility with 4.85% RSD and good stability suggest the possibility of quantitative analysis. As low as 10−11 M Hg2+ ions can be detected by CDs/Ag nanoparticles SERS fiber probes, with a concentration range of 10−5 M to 10−11 M and good selectivity. The UV-Vis absorption, XPS, and EDS mapping analyses confirm that Hg2+ can be reduced by CDs, forming a thin mercury layer that wraps around the surface of CDs/Ag nanoparticles, which results in the attenuation of the LSPR effect and Raman intensity. Furthermore, Hg2+ detection in real water samples using SERS and commercial ICP-MS shows similar results, which confirm the reliability of the fiber probes and measurement method. This research provides a novel way for detecting Hg2+ ions, with potential applications in environment, food and biology analysis.

Wang Tianyi: Data curation, Investigation. Wang Yan: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology. Han Xiaoyu: Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Zhao Xiujian: Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Han Jianjun: Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Xie Jun: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Zhang Jihong: Conceptualization, Methodology, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by, the project of Sanya Yazhou Bay Science and Technology City (SCKJ-JYRC-2022-44) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (WUT: 2023III007XXH). The author Jihong Zhang would like to thank Ms. Utada Hikaru, for her beautiful songs inspired this research.
